sections.heading: Definition
sections.body: The gap between the center and the guard on either side. Inside Zone and Power attack the A-gap. Before the snap, the QB counts defenders in the A and B gaps to decide run or pass.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.body: The gap between the guard and the tackle. Power and Counter target the B-gap with a pulling guard. The 3-technique defensive tackle is the primary B-gap defender.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.body: The gap between the tackle and the tight end (or just outside the tackle). Outside Zone and Pin and Pull attack the C-gap and the edge.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.body: The gap outside the tight end. Pin and Pull and Outside Zone attack the D-gap and the edge. If the defense overloads the edge, the answer is to cut back inside.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.body: The space between offensive linemen. Every run play targets a gap, and every defender is assigned a gap. The first rule of defense: every gap must be covered. Find the uncovered gap and that is where the ball goes.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.body: The defensive alignment system. Even numbers (0, 2, 4, 6) are head-up on a lineman. Odd numbers (1, 3, 5, 7, 9) are on the outside shoulder. An 'i' (2i, 4i) means inside shoulder. A 3-technique tackle signals an Over or Under front.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.body: A defensive tackle on the outside shoulder of the guard, attacking the B-gap. The most disruptive interior alignment. Its side tells you the front: 3-tech to the tight end is an Over front, 3-tech away is an Under front.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.body: A defender aligned to the inside or outside of an offensive player rather than head-up. Inside shade usually means inside-gap responsibility, outside shade means outside-gap.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.body: An offensive lineman is covered if a defender is head-up or in his playside gap, uncovered if no defender is there. Every blocking scheme starts with this read: an uncovered lineman helps his neighbor before climbing to the linebacker.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.body: How many running backs and tight ends are on the field, written as two digits. 11 = 1 back, 1 tight end, 3 receivers (the modern base). 12 = 2 tight ends. 21 = 2 backs. Use personnel to force the defense's hand: 11 forces a lighter Nickel box, 12 forces base.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.body: When the ball is on a hash, the field is the wide side (more room) and the boundary is the short side. Defenses put extra help to the field. Split-field coverages play one coverage to each side.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.body: A defender's position relative to the receiver: inside, outside, or head-up. A pre-snap tell: inside leverage often means zone (protecting the zone), outside leverage often means man (mirroring the receiver).

sections.heading: Definition
sections.body: The middle linebacker, and the defender the offense identifies to set pass protection. Every blocking assignment is built off the Mike ID. A wrong Mike call is the most common cause of a free rusher.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.body: The fifth defensive back, who covers the slot receiver. The most important modern defensive position. He must cover the slot in man, set the edge on runs, and tackle in space.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.body: A run-blocking family where the whole line steps in one direction and works combination blocks, displacing defenders sideways. Nobody has a specific man, each lineman is responsible for an area. Used in Inside Zone and Outside Zone.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.body: A run-blocking family built on angles and pulling linemen. Playside linemen block down while a backside lineman pulls and leads through the hole. The rule is Gap, Down, Backer. Used in Power and Counter.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.body: A run-blocking family with the simplest rule in football: covered linemen pin (down block), uncovered linemen pull to the edge. Floods the perimeter with blockers. Shares blocking with Jet Sweep.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.body: A pulling blocker who hits the edge defender on his inside shoulder and drives him outward, creating a running lane inside him. If the edge defender squeezes, the back bounces outside.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.body: The foundational run play. Zone-blocked: the back presses the line, reads the double teams, and makes one decisive cut. The base for RPOs and Zone Read. If you install one run play, make it this.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.body: The horizontal complement to Inside Zone. The line reach-steps to seal defenders inside while the back aims for the edge and reads bend, bang, or bounce off the playside tackle's block.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.body: The foundational gap-scheme run. Playside linemen block down, a back or H-back kicks out the edge, and the backside guard pulls and leads through the B-gap. Pairs with Counter.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.body: Power's misdirection twin. Same down-block scheme, but two pullers and the back takes a false step away before cutting back. Run Inside Zone or Power to train the linebackers to flow, then hit Counter the other way.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.body: Inside Zone with a QB decision attached. The backside defensive end is left unblocked and read: if he crashes on the back, the QB keeps and runs the edge, if he stays wide, the QB gives. Add an RPO tag for a triple option.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.body: A read option on Power blocking. The QB reads the unblocked end: crash equals keep, contain equals give to the back. The pulling guard leads instead of kicking out.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.body: The line blocks a run while the QB reads one defender after the snap to decide hand off or throw. That defender (the conflict defender) has two jobs and cannot do both.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.body: The one defender a play forces to choose between two responsibilities. Every passing concept names its conflict defender: Stick reads the flat defender, Smash reads the corner, Dagger reads the deep safety. You throw based on what he does.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.body: Middle Of Field Closed (one deep safety) vs Middle Of Field Open (two deep safeties). The QB's first pre-snap read. One high safety (MOFC), throw outside. Two high safeties (MOFO), throw inside. This single read drives every passing decision.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.body: Counting the deep safeties before the snap. 1 high is MOFC, throw outside. 2 high is MOFO, throw inside. 0 high means throw immediately or run. The first read on every play.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.body: The area a coverage leaves open. Every coverage has at least one void. The QB's job is to identify the shell and throw to its void. The Coverages track lists the void for each coverage.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.body: The second pre-snap read after the safety count. Tells: off corners with eyes on the QB equal zone, press corners with eyes on the receiver equal man. The cheat code is motion, if a defender follows it across the formation, it is man.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.body: A quick route adjustment when a blitz is detected. The receiver runs a slant or hitch for an immediate throw. The hot route is the extra blocker: it replaces a blocker the protection cannot account for.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.body: A quick concept: slot runs a 5 to 6 yard hitch, a back or tight end runs the flat, the outside receiver clears. Read the flat defender. If he expands to the flat, throw the stick, if he sits, throw the flat. Works against every coverage.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.body: A quick man-beater: outside receiver runs a slant, slot or back runs the flat. Read the flat defender. If he widens, throw the slant, if he sits on the slant, throw the flat. The go-to answer against Cover 1 and Cover 0.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.body: An intermediate concept: outside receiver runs a 5 yard hitch, slot runs a corner route. Read the corner. Squat on the hitch, throw the corner over him, bail to the corner, throw the hitch. The Cover 2 killer.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.body: A deep concept: a vertical clears the safety, the outside receiver runs a deep dig at 15 to 18 yards. Read the deep middle safety. Jump the dig, throw the post behind him, stay deep, throw the dig. The Cover 3 killer.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.body: Two shallow crossers cross at the mesh point, creating natural picks. The best man-coverage beater in football. Against zone, the crossers sit in the void between zones. Call it when the opponent switches to man.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.body: The spot where two crossing receivers' paths intersect on Drive or Mesh. Against man, defenders collide or get rubbed off here, springing a crosser free.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.body: A quick concept: five receivers spread horizontally at 0, 5, 8, 5, 0 yards. Three or four underneath defenders cannot cover five windows, so someone is always open. The ultimate zone killer.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.body: Four receivers run vertical routes splitting the deep zones. Read the safety count: 1 high, throw the seam away from the safety, 2 high, throw the seam between the safeties.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.body: The post pulls the deep safety inside, the wheel runs behind him up the sideline. Read the deep-half safety: jump the post, throw the wheel, stay on the wheel, throw the post. A Cover 2 deep killer.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.body: The most common protection. One side slides (zone blocks a gap), the other side blocks man. The center makes the Mike ID that splits the two halves. Vulnerable to an overloaded man side, answered with a hot route.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.body: All five linemen slide one direction, each responsible for a gap. The back checks the backside edge. Stunt-proof because nobody follows a man, but vulnerable to numbers to the slide side.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.body: Big On Big: linemen block linemen, backs and tight ends block linebackers and edge rushers. Pure man protection, keeps maximum route runners. Vulnerable to stunts, answered with a switch call.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.body: Two or more defensive linemen swapping rush lanes after the snap to confuse the blocking. Beats man protection (the blocker follows his man, the looper hits the open gap). Beaten by Full Slide or a switch call.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.body: Sending more rushers than the offense can block. Every blitz trades a coverage defender for a rusher. The reward is a sack, the risk is an open receiver. Disguise is what makes a blitz work.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.body: A zone blitz: rush five, drop three deep and three under, often dropping a lineman into coverage (the dropper). Pressure with a Cover 3 shell behind it, the best risk-reward blitz. Beaten by quick game and hot routes.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.body: A conditional blitz: a defender covering a back or tight end rushes only if his man stays in to block. If the man releases, he covers. Beaten by releasing the back on a wheel or angle into space.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.body: Any offensive player moving before the snap. Three jobs: diagnose the coverage (a defender following equals man), create a numbers advantage, and multiply plays. The five types are Jet, Orbit, Shift, Trade, and Return.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.body: A receiver sprinting full speed across the formation, snapped as he passes the QB. One motion creates five plays: jet sweep, jet fake plus Inside Zone, jet fake plus Counter, jet fake plus play-action, jet fake plus screen.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.body: Cornerback alignment at the line, jamming the receiver at the snap to disrupt route timing. Strong against quick game and slants. The risk is getting beaten deep by a fast receiver.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.body: An inside receiver off the line, between the outside receiver and the formation. The most targeted spot in football, which is why the Nickelback exists to cover it.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.body: The outside of the line, past the tackle or tight end. Outside Zone and Pin and Pull attack the edge. Read the edge defender: if he sets the edge, cut back, if he crashes, bounce outside.
